title: The weaner-to-bacon arc
subtitle: The full growing season from collecting two outdoor weaners in spring to delivering them to the abattoir at bacon weight in autumn.
excerpt: A pair of outdoor weaners collected in April and grown on to 85 kg deadweight will be ready for the abattoir in September or October. The keeper's job in between is to keep the fence hot, the water flowing, the feed consistent, and the ground rotated before it becomes a worm reservoir. This pattern maps the five-month arc from first day to last.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Pork, Outdoor Pig Production guide. https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003). https://www.gov.uk

content.text: A pair of outdoor weaners is a five-month commitment: predictable inputs, predictable outputs, no overwintering, and a freezer full of pork at the end. The main variable is the ground. Pigs that stay on the same patch for five months destroy it and build up a worm burden that limits future use. Rotation is not optional: it is what makes the system repeatable year on year.
A well-grown weaner doubles its weight in the first four weeks on your holding, from around 20 kg to 40 kg. After that, growth continues at 500 to 700 g per day on good rations. At

(90 to 110 kg live), the conversion of feed to muscle slows and fat deposition accelerates. Taking pigs significantly over 110 kg live weight produces a carcass with a high fat percentage relative to lean yield.

Each week: check fence voltage with a tester. Check and clear the water trough. Muck out the arc or add fresh straw on the deep-litter system. Observe growth rate visually: both pigs should be similar in size and condition. If one falls significantly behind the other, consult a vet: dominance-driven under-feeding is common and easy to miss in a small group.
Book the abattoir at least five to six weeks before your target date. Calculate backwards from

target: if you want 80 kg deadweight, you need approximately 107 kg live weight, which at a growth rate of 600 g per day means knowing roughly how many days remain. Tape measures calibrated for pig live weight are available and more reliable than visual guesses at heavier weights.

attrs.body: The eAML2 to slaughter is a different document from the arrival eAML2: it is a movement from your holding to the abattoir. Raise it through the APHA eAML2 system 24 to 48 hours before the move. The abattoir will ask for your herd number (derived from your CPH) on the paperwork. Keep a copy: it forms part of your pig register.
attrs.title: The movement licence to the abattoir
